contentHtml:
When Healing Looks Like Regression

What if the path to healing doesn’t always feel like moving forward? What if, sometimes, the most profound moments of growth look and feel like you’re going backward, falling apart, losing the very ground you thought you’d gained? This is the question that haunts so many on a genuine path of awakening. We are conditioned to see progress as a straight line, an accumulation of wins, a steady climb toward a well-lit summit. But the soul’s journey is not a corporate ladder. It is a wild, untamable river, and sometimes, it must flow backward to reclaim parts of itself that were left behind in the drought-stricken lowlands of our past.

The Unraveling is Not the End

We are sold a linear, sanitized story of healing. A neat, upward trajectory from broken to whole, from confused to clear. But the body, in its infinite and primal wisdom, knows nothing of straight lines. It knows cycles, seasons, the ebb and flow of tides, the spiraling dance of a galaxy. When you commit to deep, embodied healing—not the performative, Instagram-friendly version, but the real, gut-wrenching work—you are not signing up for a gentle ascent. You are agreeing to an unraveling. A holy demolition. The foundation of the person you thought you were must be reduced to rubble to make space for the raw, undeniable truth of who you have always been beneath the layers of conditioning, trauma, and social expectation. This is not a renovation; it is a complete teardown and rebuild from the sacred ground of your own being.

This unraveling can feel like utter chaos, a descent into madness. Old, destructive patterns you thought you’d conquered resurface with a terrifying vengeance, mocking your progress. Emotions you were certain you’d processed come roaring back to life, flooding your system with the force of a tsunami. The carefully constructed dam of your composure, the one that got you through the day and made you “functional,” cracks and shatters under the immense pressure. The floodwaters of grief, rage, and primordial terror you’ve held back for a lifetime begin to pour through, inundating every corner of your life. It’s easy, in these moments, to mistake this for failure. To believe you’ve lost your way, that all your work was for nothing. But this is not the end of your healing; it is the searing, undeniable beginning of your liberation. It is the soul’s fierce grace, burning away everything that is not true, everything that was built on a foundation of fear and self-abandonment.

The Body Remembers What the Mind Forgets

The mind is a masterful storyteller, a cunning editor, and a professional dissociator. It can construct elaborate narratives to explain away our pain, to justify our numbness, to keep us safe in a gilded prison of our own making. But the body is a living archive, a sacred text written in the language of sensation, tremor, and breath. It holds the perfect, unedited imprint of every experience, every joy, every violation, every moment of connection and abandonment. It is the high-fidelity recording of your life, and it never lies. When we finally begin to feel safe enough to truly inhabit our bodies—to descend from the noisy attic of the intellect into the vibrant, pulsing, and sometimes painful temple of our flesh—this archive opens. And it does not speak in polite, measured whispers.

Somatic memory is not an intellectual recollection. It is a full-body reliving. The sudden, inexplicable trembling in your hands as you reach for your partner, a ghost of a past terror. The knot of dread that tightens in your stomach when you receive a promotion, the echo of a belief that you are not worthy of success. The wave of profound, untethered sadness that washes over you on a perfectly beautiful afternoon, the grief of a younger self you had to leave behind. This is the body speaking its truth, finally given a voice. It is not regression; it is revelation. It is the past reaching into the present, not to haunt you, but to be held, witnessed, and finally healed by you. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen this pattern dozens of times. A client will come to me, distraught, saying, “I thought I was over this. Why is it all coming back now?” It’s coming back because you’re finally safe enough to feel it. The ‘regression’ is a profound sign of trust—your nervous system is beginning to believe that you, the conscious adult, are finally present and resourced enough to meet the raw, undigested experiences of the past without being annihilated. It’s an invitation to meet the wounded, exiled parts of yourself not with judgment or fear, but with the fierce, unwavering presence of your own love.

What you can feel, you can heal.

The Tyranny of the “Healing” Narrative

The modern spiritual marketplace has co-opted the language of healing and turned it into another performance metric. It has become a new form of perfectionism, draped in organic linen and smelling of palo santo. We are told to be positive, to look for the silver lining, to rush toward a premature and therefore false forgiveness. This relentless pressure to perform “healing” correctly is a subtle but profound violence. It forces us to bypass the messy, uncomfortable, raw, and often terrifying reality of what it means to truly mend a shattered soul. Real healing is not about slapping a coat of spiritual gold paint over our cracks and calling it kintsugi. It is about having the raw courage to enter the cracks themselves, to feel their jagged edges with the fingertips of our awareness, and to discover the brilliant, holy light that has been trapped within them all along. It is about excavating the wound, not just decorating it.

Emily Nagoski’s work on the stress response cycle is a crucial piece of this puzzle, a scientific validation of what the body has always known. She explains that while we can eliminate an external stressor (the tiger is gone), the stress itself remains locked in the body until we complete the physiological cycle. The shaking, the spontaneous tears, the primal scream you’ve been swallowing down for decades—these are not signs of weakness or instability. They are the body’s innate, biological mechanisms for discharging trapped survival stress and returning to a state of equilibrium and wholeness. When these powerful impulses arise, your work is not to suppress them, pathologize them, or apologize for them. Your work is to create a safe and the structure that holds the work—a bedroom with a locked door, a car in an empty parking lot, a trusted therapist’s office—where they can finally be honored, expressed, and completed. This is the path of embodied liberation. It is messy, undigified, and anything but marketable.

Love That Doesn’t Flinch

When you are in the throes of this holy unraveling, it can be terrifying for both you and your partner. The person they thought they knew, the predictable and managed version of you, is dissolving before their eyes. In its place, the raw, untamed, and gloriously alive energy of your true self is emerging. This is a profound and terrifying test of love. Can they stay present with your chaos without trying to fix it? Can they hold you in your righteous rage withoutmaking it about their own comfort? Can they witness your deepest, most desolate grief without rushing to cheer you up or distract you? Real love doesn't flinch. It tells the truth and anticipates the resulting storm. It meets your wildness not with fear, but with a deep, unwavering presence that says, “I am here. I am not going anywhere.”

This is not about finding a partner who will save you or be your therapist. That is a codependent trap that dishonors both of you. It is about finding a partner who is brave enough to stand in the fire with you, not to pull you out, but to trust that you have everything you need to find your own way through. It is about a love that is not predicated on you being easy, convenient, or predictable. It is a love that is fiercely devoted to your freedom, even when that freedom looks like tearing your own world apart. And let’s be brutally clear: Love does not require you to be sucked into someone else's void. This is about mutual respect for the sovereign, and often messy, path of each individual soul. A partner who is doing their own work will be able to witness your storm without getting swept away by it, because they are anchored in the ground of their own being. They have their own roots, their own connection to the earth, and they are not looking to you to be their source of stability.

The wound is not the problem. The hiding is.

The Difference Between a Wound and a Weapon

As you navigate this treacherous and sacred territory, it is crucial to learn the subtle but vital distinction between the authentic expression of your pain and the unconscious weaponization of it. Your trauma is not your fault. Let that land deep in your bones. It is not your fault. But your healing is, and always will be, your responsibility. When your past is triggered and a tidal wave of emotion surges through you, it is your work to hold yourself, to feel the feelings fully, and to communicate your needs with as much clarity and ownership as you can muster. It is not a free pass to lash out, to manipulate, to punish, or to demand that your partner walk on eggshells around your triggers. A client once described this as learning to “hold the hot coal” of her own pain instead of immediately throwing it at her husband. This is the practice. It is the hard, holy, and deeply rewarding work of emotional maturity. It is learning to say, “I am feeling a tremendous amount of anger right now, and it is not about you. I need some space to be with it.”

When you begin to consistently take responsibility for your own healing, a remarkable thing happens. Your perception changes. You stop trying to pull others into your storm — and you stay in your lane. You learn to honor the wisdom of your body’s “no” as a sacred boundary, not as a judgment on the other person. You learn to ask for what you need without apology or aggression, from a place of centered self-worth. You become a safe harbor for yourself, and in doing so, you naturally become a safer and more magnetic partner for the one you love. And a word of advice from the trenches, learned through hard experience: If they've got a tattoo of a genderless inner child floating in a pink bubble… run. This isn't about judgment; it's about recognizing the signs of someone who has mistaken spiritual aesthetics for the grueling, unglamorous work of actual embodiment. True depth is rarely so well-branded.

The Sacred Pause: Your Gateway to Freedom

In moments of intense activation, when the past feels more real than the present, the most powerful and compassionate tool you have is the pause. The sacred space between the trigger and your habitual reaction. In that space, however brief, lies your freedom. It is the moment where you can choose to turn toward yourself instead of away from yourself. It is the choice to place a hand on your heart and breathe, a simple, profound act of self-communion. It is the opportunity to whisper to the terrified, young part of you, “I am here. I feel you. I am not leaving.” This is not about suppressing your emotions or forcing yourself to be calm. It is about creating a container of presence, a field of loving awareness, within which the storm of emotion can move, express, and ultimately transform without causing harm.

This pause is an act of profound devotion, a concept central to both Tantra and Vedanta. In Vedanta, this is the practice of becoming the Sakshi, the silent, unmoving witness to the play of your own inner world, the drama of the mind and body. You are not the anger; you are the awareness that perceives the anger. You are not the fear; you are the space that holds the fear. This is a radical shift in identification. It is a declaration that your inner landscape is worthy of your full, non-judgmental attention. It is the foundation of a new relationship with yourself, one based not on control and suppression, but on radical trust and intimacy. With each conscious breath you take in that sacred pause, you are rewriting the neurological scripts of your past and reclaiming the sovereign power of your present moment. You are moving from reaction to response, from bondage to liberation.

Your morning routine is not a spiritual practice if it is just another performance.

The Great Integration: Returning to Wholeness

The unraveling, for all its intensity, is not the final destination. After the storm comes the quiet. After the holy demolition comes the slow, intentional, and deeply satisfying work of rebuilding. This is the integration phase. It is where you begin to lovingly weave the fragmented, exiled pieces of yourself back into a cohesive, vibrant whole. It is where you learn to live in this new, more authentic skin, which may feel exquisitely tender and unfamiliar at first. This phase is often less dramatic than the unraveling, but it is no less important. It is the long, slow, patient journey of learning to trust your own body again, to honor your own unique rhythms, and to navigate the world not from a place of fear-based strategy, but from a place of deep, embodied knowing. It is learning to trust the subtle cues of your nervous system, the wisdom of your gut, the language of your heart.

Here, you discover, in your own bones, that the regression was actually a pilgrimage. A necessary journey into the underworld of your own being to retrieve the lost treasures of your soul—your power, your innocence, your voice, your wildness. You return not as the same person who descended, but as someone more whole, more real, more fiercely and tenderly alive. You learn that your scars are not signs of damage, but luminous maps of your resilience. They are the sacred testimony of your courage to feel, to break open, and to heal from the inside out. And as you stand in this new, solid landscape of your own being, you may find that the love, safety, and validation you once desperately sought from others is now a wellspring within you. The ground you once searched for outside yourself is now the unshakable foundation of your own presence. The path forward is not a return to who you were, but a continual, glorious unfolding into the magnificent, mysterious, and ever-deepening truth of who you are becoming. May you touch with intention and receive without armor.


This site provides educational content about conscious intimacy and sacred sexuality. It is not therapy. For sexual trauma, consult a licensed somatic or sex therapist.
